questions.stem: The Michaelis-Menten rate law for an enzyme-catalysed reaction is
questions.explanation: The Michaelis-Menten equation is $v = \dfrac{V_{\max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}$, a rectangular hyperbola in the substrate concentration $[S]$. It rises linearly at low $[S]$ and saturates at $V_{\max}$ for large $[S]$.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: In the Michaelis-Menten law $v = \dfrac{V_{\max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}$, the maximum velocity $V_{\max}$ is approached when
questions.explanation: As $[S] \to \infty$, the fraction $\dfrac{[S]}{K_m + [S]} \to 1$, so $v \to V_{\max}$. The enzyme is then saturated: essentially all of it is bound as the ES complex and the rate cannot increase further.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: The Michaelis constant $K_m$ equals the substrate concentration at which the reaction velocity is
questions.explanation: Set $[S] = K_m$ in the rate law: $v = \dfrac{V_{\max} K_m}{K_m + K_m} = \dfrac{V_{\max} K_m}{2K_m} = \dfrac{V_{\max}}{2}$. Thus $K_m$ is the half-saturation constant, the substrate level giving half the maximum velocity.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: An enzyme has $V_{\max} = 100\ \mu\text{M/min}$ and $K_m = 20\ \mu\text{M}$. The reaction velocity at $[S] = 20\ \mu\text{M}$ is
questions.explanation: At $[S] = K_m = 20$, the velocity is half-maximal: $v = \dfrac{100 \times 20}{20 + 20} = \dfrac{2000}{40} = 50\ \mu\text{M/min} = V_{\max}/2$.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: With $V_{\max} = 100\ \mu\text{M/min}$ and $K_m = 20\ \mu\text{M}$, the velocity at $[S] = 60\ \mu\text{M}$ is
questions.explanation: $v = \dfrac{V_{\max}[S]}{K_m + [S]} = \dfrac{100 \times 60}{20 + 60} = \dfrac{6000}{80} = 75\ \mu\text{M/min}$.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: At very low substrate concentration ($[S] \ll K_m$), the Michaelis-Menten rate law reduces to
questions.explanation: When $[S] \ll K_m$, the denominator $K_m + [S] \approx K_m$, so $v \approx \dfrac{V_{\max}}{K_m}[S]$. The rate is then proportional to $[S]$, i.e. first order in substrate, with apparent rate constant $V_{\max}/K_m$.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: At very high substrate concentration ($[S] \gg K_m$), the reaction order with respect to substrate is
questions.explanation: When $[S] \gg K_m$, $v \approx V_{\max}$, a constant independent of $[S]$. A rate independent of substrate concentration is zero order: the enzyme is saturated and adding more substrate does not speed the reaction.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: The standard mechanism is $E + S \underset{k_{-1}}{\overset{k_1}{\rightleftharpoons}} ES \overset{k_2}{\rightarrow} E + P$. The quasi-steady-state approximation assumes that
options: the complex concentration $[ES]$ changes slowly, so $\frac{d[ES]}{dt} \approx 0$
questions.explanation: After a brief transient the intermediate $ES$ is formed and broken down at nearly equal rates, so its concentration is approximately constant: $\frac{d[ES]}{dt} \approx 0$. This quasi-steady-state assumption is what allows the Michaelis-Menten law to be derived.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: Applying the quasi-steady-state approximation to $E + S \underset{k_{-1}}{\overset{k_1}{\rightleftharpoons}} ES \overset{k_2}{\rightarrow} E + P$ gives the Michaelis constant
questions.explanation: Setting $\frac{d[ES]}{dt} = k_1[E][S] - (k_{-1} + k_2)[ES] = 0$ and using $[E] = E_0 - [ES]$ leads to $K_m = \dfrac{k_{-1} + k_2}{k_1}$. It combines the breakdown rates of $ES$ (back to $E+S$ and forward to product) over the formation rate.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: In terms of the total enzyme concentration $E_0$ and catalytic constant $k_2$ (also written $k_{cat}$), the maximum velocity is
questions.explanation: At saturation essentially all enzyme is bound, $[ES] \approx E_0$, and product forms at rate $k_2[ES]$. Hence $V_{\max} = k_2 E_0$ (often $k_{cat} E_0$). It scales linearly with the amount of enzyme present.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: An enzyme has turnover number $k_2 = 50\ \text{s}^{-1}$ and total concentration $E_0 = 2\ \mu\text{M}$. Its $V_{\max} = k_2 E_0$ is
questions.explanation: $V_{\max} = k_2 E_0 = 50\ \text{s}^{-1} \times 2\ \mu\text{M} = 100\ \mu\text{M/s}$. This is the rate when the enzyme is fully saturated with substrate.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: The Lineweaver-Burk (double-reciprocal) form of the Michaelis-Menten equation is
questions.explanation: Taking reciprocals of $v = \dfrac{V_{\max}[S]}{K_m+[S]}$ gives $\dfrac{1}{v} = \dfrac{K_m + [S]}{V_{\max}[S]} = \dfrac{K_m}{V_{\max}}\dfrac{1}{[S]} + \dfrac{1}{V_{\max}}$. Plotting $1/v$ against $1/[S]$ gives a straight line, which is the value of the linearization.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: On a Lineweaver-Burk plot of $1/v$ against $1/[S]$, the vertical-axis intercept (where $1/[S] = 0$) equals
questions.explanation: From $\dfrac{1}{v} = \dfrac{K_m}{V_{\max}}\dfrac{1}{[S]} + \dfrac{1}{V_{\max}}$, setting $1/[S] = 0$ leaves $\dfrac{1}{v} = \dfrac{1}{V_{\max}}$. So the $y$-intercept is $1/V_{\max}$, from which $V_{\max}$ is read off.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: On a Lineweaver-Burk plot, the slope of the line is $K_m/V_{\max}$ and the $y$-intercept is $1/V_{\max}$. The horizontal-axis intercept (where $1/v = 0$) is
questions.explanation: Set $1/v = 0$: $\dfrac{K_m}{V_{\max}}\dfrac{1}{[S]} + \dfrac{1}{V_{\max}} = 0$, so $\dfrac{1}{[S]} = -\dfrac{1}{K_m}$. The $x$-intercept is $-1/K_m$, giving $K_m$ directly from where the line crosses the horizontal axis.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: A reaction reaches half its maximum velocity at a substrate concentration of $5\ \text{mM}$. The Michaelis constant $K_m$ is
questions.explanation: By definition $v = V_{\max}/2$ exactly when $[S] = K_m$. Since half-maximal velocity occurs at $[S] = 5\ \text{mM}$, we read off $K_m = 5\ \text{mM}$ directly.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: A small $K_m$ relative to another enzyme acting on the same substrate indicates
options: higher affinity for the substrate (half-saturation reached at lower $[S]$)
questions.explanation: A small $K_m$ means the enzyme reaches half its maximum rate at a low substrate concentration, so it binds substrate effectively even when scarce. Hence small $K_m$ is associated with high substrate affinity. $K_m$ and $V_{\max}$ are independent parameters.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: With $V_{\max} = 90\ \mu\text{M/min}$ and $K_m = 30\ \mu\text{M}$, the velocity at $[S] = 10\ \mu\text{M}$ is
questions.explanation: $v = \dfrac{90 \times 10}{30 + 10} = \dfrac{900}{40} = 22.5\ \mu\text{M/min}$. Since $[S] = 10 < K_m = 30$, the velocity is well below half-maximal ($45$).
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: The catalytic efficiency of an enzyme is commonly measured by the ratio
questions.explanation: At low substrate the rate is $\dfrac{V_{\max}}{K_m}[S] = \dfrac{k_2 E_0}{K_m}[S]$, so the second-order rate constant per unit enzyme is $k_2/K_m$ (or $k_{cat}/K_m$). It captures both binding ($K_m$) and turnover ($k_2$) and is the standard measure of catalytic efficiency.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: The Michaelis-Menten velocity curve $v$ versus $[S]$ has the shape of
options: a straight line through the origin
questions.explanation: $v = \dfrac{V_{\max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}$ rises steeply at first, then bends over and levels off toward the horizontal asymptote $v = V_{\max}$. This is a rectangular hyperbola, the characteristic saturating shape of enzyme kinetics.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: An enzyme follows Michaelis-Menten kinetics with $K_m = 4\ \text{mM}$. At what substrate concentration is the velocity equal to $80\%$ of $V_{\max}$?
questions.explanation: Set $\dfrac{[S]}{K_m + [S]} = 0.8$, so $[S] = 0.8K_m + 0.8[S]$, giving $0.2[S] = 0.8K_m$ and $[S] = 4K_m = 4 \times 4 = 16\ \text{mM}$. Reaching 80% of $V_{\max}$ needs four times $K_m$.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: Total (conserved) enzyme in the standard mechanism satisfies
questions.explanation: Enzyme is neither created nor destroyed; it is only shuttled between free form $E$ and bound complex $ES$. So $[E] + [ES] = E_0$, the total enzyme concentration, is conserved throughout. This relation closes the system when applying the quasi-steady-state approximation.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: An enzyme obeys $v = \dfrac{V_{\max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}$ with $V_{\max} = 120\ \mu\text{M/min}$ and $K_m = 40\ \mu\text{M}$.
questions.solution: When $[S] = K_m$ the velocity is exactly half-maximal, namely $v =$ {{1}} uM/min. When $[S] = 120\ \mu\text{M}$ (three times $K_m$) the velocity is $v =$ {{2}} uM/min. As $[S]$ grows without bound, $v$ approaches {{3}} uM/min.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: Consider the limiting regimes of the Michaelis-Menten law $v = \dfrac{V_{\max}[S]}{K_m+[S]}$.
questions.solution: When the substrate is scarce, $[S] \ll K_m$, the rate is approximately proportional to $[S]$, so the reaction is {{1}} order in substrate. When the substrate is abundant, $[S] \gg K_m$, the rate levels off at $V_{\max}$, so the reaction is {{2}} order in substrate. The crossover between these regimes happens around $[S] = K_m$, where the velocity is the fraction {{3}} of $V_{\max}$.
questions.explanation: Low $[S]$: $v \approx (V_{\max}/K_m)[S]$, first order. High $[S]$: $v \approx V_{\max}$, zero order. At $[S] = K_m$, $v = V_{\max}/2$, i.e. the fraction $0.5$ of the maximum.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: An enzyme assay gives $V_{\max} = 200\ \mu\text{M/min}$ and $K_m = 25\ \mu\text{M}$. A Lineweaver-Burk plot of $1/v$ against $1/[S]$ is drawn.
questions.solution: The $y$-intercept of the line is $1/V_{\max} =$ {{1}} (in min/uM, to four decimal places). The slope of the line is $K_m/V_{\max} =$ {{2}} (to four decimal places). The $x$-intercept is at $1/[S] =$ {{3}} per uM.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: The catalytic mechanism is $E + S \underset{k_{-1}}{\overset{k_1}{\rightleftharpoons}} ES \overset{k_2}{\rightarrow} E + P$ with total enzyme $E_0$.
questions.solution: The approximation used to derive the rate law sets the time derivative of $[ES]$ to {{1}}. Under it the Michaelis constant works out to $K_m = (k_{-1} + k_2)/k_1$, and the maximum velocity is $V_{\max} = k_2 E_0$. If $k_2 = 30\ \text{s}^{-1}$ and $E_0 = 5\ \mu\text{M}$, then $V_{\max} =$ {{2}} uM/s.
questions.explanation: The quasi-steady-state approximation sets $d[ES]/dt = 0$. With $k_2 = 30$ and $E_0 = 5$, $V_{\max} = k_2 E_0 = 30 \times 5 = 150\ \mu\text{M/s}$.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: Two enzymes A and B act on the same substrate. Enzyme A has $K_m = 2\ \mu\text{M}$ and enzyme B has $K_m = 50\ \mu\text{M}$.
questions.solution: Because a smaller $K_m$ means half-saturation is reached at a lower substrate level, enzyme {{1}} has the higher affinity for the substrate. At a substrate concentration well below $2\ \mu\text{M}$, the enzyme that operates closer to its first-order (substrate-limited) regime, since $[S] \ll K_m$ for it, is enzyme {{2}}.
questions.explanation: Enzyme A, with the smaller $K_m = 2$, binds substrate more tightly, so it has the higher affinity. Enzyme B has the larger $K_m = 50$, so for a given low $[S]$ the ratio $[S]/K_m$ is smallest for B, keeping it furthest into the first-order regime.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology

questions.stem: An enzyme follows Michaelis-Menten kinetics with $K_m = 6\ \text{mM}$ and $V_{\max} = 60\ \mu\text{M/min}$.
questions.solution: At $[S] = 6\ \text{mM}$ the velocity equals half of $V_{\max}$, namely {{1}} uM/min. To reach a velocity of $0.75 V_{\max}$, the substrate concentration must be $[S] = 3K_m =$ {{2}} mM.
questions.explanation: At $[S] = K_m$, $v = V_{\max}/2 = 30$. For $v = 0.75 V_{\max}$: $\dfrac{[S]}{K_m+[S]} = 0.75 \Rightarrow [S] = 3K_m = 3 \times 6 = 18\ \text{mM}$.
questions.source: in the style of Murray, Mathematical Biology
